feedback.text: The main points of this legislation are great - they have been a big problem for a long time. Unfortunately they do not cover all issues. I would like if the DFA also covered: 1. Advertising disguised as legitimate posts: Many social media sites (Facebook, Twitter, Reddit) have sponsored post that are barely disclosed with a small bit of text, in the middle of your normal feed. This is especially harrowing with Google and Amazon (and many other online shopping sites) where paid ads appear at the top of the search results, even if they are irrelevant or not the best. This is actually a big issue both for the buyers and the sellers using the platform. Even if they offer a good, well reviewed and sought after product, they still need to pay for ads so that they aren't drowned out by garbage. 2. A few different, but intertwined issues - Right to repair, DRM and remote killswitches: Modern computer networking and hardware allows companies to arbitrarily force you to only use their products. E.g. Apple Iphones cannot be repaired, unless you send it to them. Even if you buy two brand new phones and swap their parts with one another, the phones will have limited functionality. The individual parts are marked with Unique IDs, and if they don't match, its a problem. Another example is taking away ownership from digital products people have bought - for example Ubisoft removing "The Crew" video game or Sony removing movies from peoples libraries. And its not just entertainment companies - JohnDeere is able to remotely shut off tractors. This is a severe food security issue. I do not even wish to mention HP printers and their horrific practices. 3. Private encrypted communications: This is more towards the EU and their relentless attacks against encrypted messaging apps. The EU claims it is to stop terrorists and pedophiles. I am neither of those, but I am politically active and also like to dress up in ways that would allow certain jurisdictions to legally apply the death penalty to me. Private communications are absolutely NON-NEGOTIABLY NECESSARY to a functional democracy. In fact, the EU itself recognizes this fact, by wanting the "Lawful intercept" technologies to not apply to certain groups of people - e.g. members of EU parliament. If such decryption technologies were implemented they would certainly be abused - either by our own "representatives" or foreign actors (like the Salt Typhoon hack on USA telecoms) Really the big issue is companies becoming gatekeepers and squeezing dry their end users, business partners and even governments. Some of them shouldn't have been allowed to become so big in the first place, since they used unethical or straight up illegal tactics to get there (e.g. Facebook having literal spyware on Snapchat users through "Project Ghostbusters" and Project Atlas before acquiring them). There are multiple ways to combat this, but to me it is clear - their isn't a business incentive to be ethical, so they aren't. I would love for DFA-like legislation to pass, for more fines to be enacted, and for alternative software to be developed. PS: VERY IMPORTANT 4th point: Limit severe (probably anti-GDPR) data collection by AI companies. Microsoft, Google, Apple (also Facebook) want to implement highly privileged access to personal data on the Operating System level. Microsoft even take literal screenshots of your screen every so often with "Copilot Recall" PPS: Online commerce is a major part of modern society. Unfortunately, a few payment processors (mostly VISA / Mastercard / PayPal etc.) control 95% of the market. This allows them to unilaterally apply vague, overreaching policies on who can participate in ANY market and ban those they deem a "risk", even if the seller, buyer AND local governments approve of the goods/services being exchanged.
